name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ (٤٥)
```

```english
And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard except for the humble (45)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ (٤٦)
```

```english
—those who are certain that they will encounter their Lord and that they will return to Him. (46)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ** — "And take recourse in patience and prayer" (2:45). Seeking assistance (الاستعانة), which is the request for aid (طلب العون), only occurs regarding important matters and calamities (النوازل) that a human being is not strong enough to handle alone. Since there is, in reality, no helper except Allah, the Glorified, then aid in important matters consists of the human being resisting them through steadfastness (الثبات), uprightness (الاستقامة), and connecting to Him, the Exalted, by turning away from all else toward Him and turning toward Him with one's very soul. This is precisely what patience (الصبر) and prayer (الصلاة) are, and they are the best means for that. For patience (الصبر) — *the soul's endurance and restraint in the face of hardship* — diminishes every great calamity that descends. Furthermore, by turning toward Allah and seeking refuge in Him, the spirit of faith (الإيمان) is awakened and becomes aware that the human being is leaning upon a pillar that can never be demolished and a means that can never be severed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخَاشِعِينَ** — "and it is indeed hard except for the humble" (2:45). The pronoun "it" (ها) refers back to prayer (الصلاة). As for referring it back to "seeking assistance" (الاستعانة) because it is implied by His saying "take recourse" (استعينوا), this is apparently contradicted by His saying "except for the humble" (إلا على الخاشعين); for humility (الخشوع) does not possess much affinity with patience (الصبر). The difference between humility (الخشوع) and submissiveness (الخضوع) — even though both contain the meaning of self-abasement (التذلل) and brokenness (الانكسار) — is that submissiveness (الخضوع) is specific to the physical limbs (الجوارح), while humility (الخشوع) is specific to the heart (القلب).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ** — "those who know that they will encounter their Lord" (2:46). In this context — namely, the context of belief in the Hereafter — it is a matter of certainty (اليقين), where conjecture (الظن) and supposition (الحسبان) that do not preclude the opposite (النقيض) are of no avail. The Exalted said: **وَبِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ** — "and are certain of the Hereafter" (2:4). It is possible that the reason for using this term here is to account for the actualization of humility (الخشوع). This is because gradual types of knowledge (العلوم التدريجية) that result from gradual causes involve the perceiving soul progressing through stages of awareness (تنبه), then doubt (شك), then the preponderance (ترجح) of one of the two contradictory sides, and then the gradual disappearance of opposing possibilities until the decisive perception (الادراك الجازم) — which is knowledge (العلم) — is completed. When this type of knowledge relates to a formidable matter that causes the soul to tremble, worry, and feel humble, the humility (الخشوع) accompanying it begins from the moment the preponderance (الرجحان) starts, even before the scientific perception (الإدراك العلمي) is attained and completed. Thus, placing "conjecture" (الظن) in the place of "knowledge" (العلم) is an indication that a human being does not require more than a mere awareness that he has a Lord Whom he might encounter and to Whom he might return. This is like the saying of the poet:

*So I said to them: "Conjecture (ظنوا) two thousand from Madhhij,*
*Their leaders clad in Persian chainmail."*

An enemy is only frightened by certainty (اليقين), not by doubt (الشك), yet he commanded them to "conjecture" (الظن) because conjecture would suffice to make them desist from opposition, without the need for certainty (اليقين), such that the one threatening would have to burden himself with producing certainty in them through explanation, without showing concern for their state. Based on this, the verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَن كَانَ يَرْجُو لِقَاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا** — "So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously" (18:110). All of this applies if the "encounter" (اللقاء) in His saying "encounter their Lord" (ملاقو ربهم) refers to the Day of Resurrection. However, if it refers to what will be illustrated in Surah al-A'raf — Allah willing — then there is no problem with the term at all.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, who said: "Whenever a matter distressed Ali عليه‌السلام, he would rise to prayer (الصلوة) and then recite this verse: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ**."

Also in *al-Kafi*: Narrated from him عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he said: "Patience (الصبر) is fasting (الصيام)." He also said: "When a severe calamity (النازلة الشديدة) descends upon a man, let him fast. Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ**, meaning fasting."

**I say:** al-Ayyashi also narrated the content of these two traditions in his *Tafsir*. The interpretation of patience (الصبر) as fasting (الصيام) is by way of identifying an instance (المصداق) and the application of a general concept to a specific case (الجري).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: Narrated from Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he said: "Patience (الصبر) is fasting (الصوم). When hardship or a calamity descends upon a man, let him fast. Indeed, Allah says: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخَاشِعِينَ**. And the humble one (الخاشع) is the one who is lowly (الذليل) in his prayer and attentive (المقبل) to it—meaning the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام."

**I say:** He عليه‌السلام derived from this the recommendation (استحباب) of fasting and prayer when hardships and difficulties descend, as well as seeking a means (التوسل) through the Prophet and the *Wali* during such times; this is the esoteric interpretation (التأويل) of fasting and prayer as the Messenger of Allah and the Commander of the Faithful.

Also in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: Narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ** (the verse), he says: "They are certain (يوقنون) that they will be resurrected, and 'conjecture' (الظن) from them is certainty (يقين)."

**I say:** al-Saduq also narrated this.

Ibn Shahrashub narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام that the verse was revealed concerning Ali, Uthman ibn Maz'un, Ammar ibn Yasir, and their companions.
